Party_Details.Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is part of the ABB Group, a worldwide group of companies that manufacture, market and sell products for industrial and commercial uses, including low, medium and high voltage circuit breakers.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is part of the Singapore component of the ABB Group, collectively referred to with other Singapore entities as the ABB Singapore Group.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is owned by ABB Asea Brown Boveri Ltd, a Swiss company, and was set up in Singapore to be the holding company of the Singapore businesses.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is the sole shareholder of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the pre-2003 employment contracts and Employee Handbook, the scope of contractual and equitable obligations of senior management personnel extended to restricting their participation in external business ventures and competitive activities involving medium voltage switchgear products.
Party_Details.Rule: Under employment and company law, the obligations of senior management and directors are determined by the express terms of their contracts and any incorporated policy documents, together with general principles requiring them to protect their employer’s assets and confidential information, avoid conflicts between personal and corporate interests, and refrain from engaging in outside business activities that may interfere with or compete with the employer’s business.
Party_Details.Application: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd issued and administered employment contracts and an Employee Handbook that set out restrictions on outside business participation, confidentiality, and conflicts of interest for senior personnel such as Sher Hock Guan Charles, and these documents formed the framework within which his subsequent conduct in relation to other companies and medium voltage product ventures would be assessed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether the Employee Handbook was contractually incorporated into successive employment contracts, interprets the scope of any obligation to promote the interests of the ABB Group, and determines which provisions are apt to be contractual terms. It concludes that certain Handbook clauses on conflicts of interest and protection of assets and information applied throughout the defendant’s employment, but that an obligation to promote the interests of the global ABB Group did not continue beyond the first contract. The court then uses these findings to frame the assessment of alleged breaches and liability.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd is part of the ABB Singapore Group and manufactures miniature circuit breakers, which are low voltage products.
ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd is a wholly owned subsidiary of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd.
From January 1997 to January 2000, Sher Hock Guan Charles was President of the second plaintiff.
From July 1998 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was a director of the second plaintiff.
Between January 1997 and January 2001, the defendant's employer was the second plaintiff.

On 15 January 2001, pursuant to a new organisation structure for the ABB Singapore Group which had come into effect on 1 January 2001, his employment was transferred from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff with retrospective effect to 1 January 2001.

The 19 July 1996 employment contract between the first plaintiff and the defendant stated that he should use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group and would serve as President of the second plaintiff.

The 20 September 1999 employment contract, effective from 1 February 2000, was on the letterhead of the first plaintiff, signed by both the first plaintiff and the second plaintiff, stated that the defendant's position was 'President' and that the employing entity was the second plaintiff, and provided that he would maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters and not divulge any information to outside members; private participation in other business activities, especially in their line of business, was strictly not allowed.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-2003 conduct of Sher Hock Guan Charles in relation to external companies and medium voltage product development created a conflict with his roles as President and director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, given that this company manufactured low voltage products.
Party_Details.Rule: Company officers and senior managers are avoid situations where their external business activities intersect with or create potential competition or conflicts with the product lines and commercial interests of the company for which they serve, as defined by their contractual terms and any incorporated policies.
Party_Details.Application: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd operated in low voltage products while Sher Hock Guan Charles held senior roles there and later maintained a directorship after his employment shifted to ABB Industry Pte Ltd, so his subsequent involvement with other entities and product segments had to be evaluated against the scope of this company’s low voltage manufacturing activities and his continuing board position.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment recognises that the defendant, as a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, occupied a fiduciary position in relation to that company, but notes that its business was confined to low voltage products and that it did not manufacture medium voltage products. The court reasons that this limits the extent to which later medium voltage activities could be connected to any breach of obligations owed to this plaintiff, and ultimately focuses most liability analysis on the third plaintiff.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Industry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Industry Pte Ltd is part of the ABB Singapore Group and carries on business as, among other things, a manufacturer of switchgears and an installer of electrical works.
ABB Industry Pte Ltd is a wholly owned subsidiary of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd.
From January 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was General Manager of the third plaintiff, ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
From September 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, third plaintiff.
With retrospective effect to 1 January 2001, his employment was transferred from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff.
After leaving the third plaintiff, the defendant joined Huadian as its General Manager; this appointment took effect on 3 March 2003.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before and up to February 2003, the conduct of Sher Hock Guan Charles in assisting Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute to identify and engage a technical adviser, in facilitating the development and commercialisation of medium voltage switchgear products through Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, and in participating in other business entities, conflicted with or was restricted by his contractual and senior management obligations to ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior managers responsible for a business area are generally expected, under contract and general company law principles, to protect their employer’s commercial interests, avoid conflicts between their own external business activities and the employer’s operations, refrain from using their position to advance competing ventures, and disclose to their employer material information about external projects that may affect the employer’s markets or product lines.
Party_Details.Application: While serving as General Manager and Vice-President responsible for low voltage products at ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles engaged with Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute about its plan to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage switchgear and circuit breakers in China and other Asian markets, introduced former ABB technical expert Guenther Leonhardt to that institute, and later joined and invested in Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, which manufactured and marketed medium voltage products internationally. During the same period he also facilitated a property development project in Xiamen through Great Vision International Pte Ltd and was involved in the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd. These pre-dispute actions created a factual context in which his external roles and the emerging medium voltage venture intersected with ABB Industry Pte Ltd’s activities in supplying switchgear products for projects, and with the contractual provisions on outside business participation and conflicts of interest.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court concludes that the defendant, as a very senior manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary duties similar to those of a director, in addition to his contractual obligations under the employment contracts and incorporated Handbook. It finds that his assistance to Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute in securing the services of Guenther Leonhardt for the development and commercialisation of medium voltage products, which were intended to be marketed in China and other Asian markets and to compete at an international level, was inconsistent with his obligation to act in the best interests of ABB Industry Pte Ltd and to avoid conflicts of interest. The court reasons that he should have informed ABB Industry Pte Ltd of XIHARI’s plans and declined to assist in recruiting a technical adviser. It also analyses his involvement with Great Vision, Xiamen Fu Cheng, Webmoney, Gelpag International and Gelpag mbH, distinguishing between activities that were unrelated to ABB Industry Pte Ltd’s business and those that contributed to the establishment and success of Huadian as a competitor. On the totality of the evidence, the court holds that he was in breach of his contractual and fiduciary obligations to ABB Industry Pte Ltd and that liability on the issue of responsibility is established, with quantum to be determined separately.

Party_Details.Name: Sher Hock Guan Charles

Facts.Text: Sher Hock Guan Charles is a Singaporean who worked for various companies in the ABB Group between 1990 and February 2003.
During his career with the ABB Group, Sher Hock Guan Charles worked in Xiamen, China for ABB China Ltd and ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd before being based in Singapore from January 1997.
From January 1997 to January 2000, Sher Hock Guan Charles held the position of President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
From July 1998 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
From January 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was General Manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

From September 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, in ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

On 19 July 1996, Sher Hock Guan Charles signed an employment contract with ABB Holdings Pte Ltd covering the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000, which stated that he would serve as President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.

On 20 September 1999, Sher Hock Guan Charles signed a second employment contract, effective from 1 February 2000, which required him to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters, not to divulge information to outside members, and stated that private participation in other business activities, especially in the company’s line of business, was strictly not allowed.

On 15 January 2001, Sher Hock Guan Charles received and accepted a letter from ABB Industry Pte Ltd transferring his employment from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd with retrospective effect from 1 January 2001, attaching a Summary of Terms and Conditions of Employment for staff category M1/M2.
From 1 January 2001, Sher Hock Guan Charles’s employer of record became ABB Industry Pte Ltd, while he continued to hold a directorship in ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.

In February 2002, after being informed by Xiamen Mayor Hong Yong Shi of a land development opportunity in Xiamen, Sher Hock Guan Charles relayed this opportunity to his brother, Sher Hock Sing, and business associate Lin Qunxing, leading to the incorporation of Great Vision International Pte Ltd on 18 February 2002 to pursue the project.

On 18 February 2002, Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated with shareholders and directors including Sher Hock Sing and two others, to undertake a property development project in Xiamen through a subsidiary company in China.

In July 2002, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd was established in China as a subsidiary of Great Vision International Pte Ltd to carry out the Xiamen property development project.

On 3 December 2002, a payment voucher of Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd recorded a cash advance of RMB70,000 described as a travel expenses loan, listing Sher Hock Guan Charles as payee and applicant, with receipt acknowledged by Huang Jianhuang.

In late October 2002, while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles attended the 10th anniversary celebrations of ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China, where he met representatives of Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute.

During the ABB Xiamen anniversary event in October 2002, officers of Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute informed Sher Hock Guan Charles that they were looking for a technical adviser for research and development projects relating to a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.

In November 2002, Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute officers, including Pei Zhengjiang and Zheng Jun, had several telephone conversations with Sher Hock Guan Charles about engaging an internationally known technical adviser and mentioned the name of former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt.

Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles and Guenther Leonhardt exchanged nine e-mails concerning Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute’s enquiries about engaging Leonhardt as a technical adviser for research and development projects on medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters and as a technical controller for a manufacturing company that would produce and market such products in China and other Asian markets.

In November 2002, Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute’s general manager, Pei Zhengjiang, met Sher Hock Guan Charles in Singapore and asked him to check with Guenther Leonhardt on the possibility of engaging Leonhardt as a technical adviser.

By December 2002, Sher Hock Guan Charles was aware that Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute intended to develop a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters and to market the resulting products in China and potentially in other Asian markets through a manufacturing company.
In November 2002, Fu Hsiang entered into a joint venture agreement with XIHARI for this purpose and Huadian was subsequently established as the joint venture company.
By the end of January 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles had decided to leave ABB Industry Pte Ltd and had informed senior ABB officer Tom Sjoekvist of his intention to resign.
By 31 January 2003, Huadian had finished prototyping and the most critical type testing. The testing of the product had begun on 25 October 2002 and was fully completed on 13 March 2003.
This appointment took effect on 3 March 2003.
On 5 or 6 March 2003, he called Mr Leonhardt and invited him to visit Huadian’s premises in Xiamen.
In late March 2003, a formal technical consultancy agreement was signed between him and Huadian but backdated to 1 March 2003.
Huadian commenced marketing its vacuum circuit breaker in July 2003 and received the first order for the same in September 2003.
On 25 April 2003, the defendant was appointed a director of Great Vision in place of his brother and also acquired part of his brother’s shareholding in Great Vision.
On 24 May 2002, Webmoney Investment (s) Pte Ltd was incorporated. The defendant became a director of this company two months after leaving the plaintiffs’ employ.
In May 2003, the defendant procured the incorporation of a company called Gelpag International Pte Ltd and became a director and shareholder of the same.

Later in 2003, the defendant sought to enhance Huadian’s association with Germany by incorporating a company in Germany for purposes of holding shares in Huadian. Gelpag mbH was incorporated in October 2003. The defendant owns 85% of the shares in Gelpag mbH whilst Mr Leonhardt owns 10% thereof.
In late March 2004, Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of the shares in Huadian at par value and the defendant was appointed a director of Huadian.
In late March 2004, Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of the shares in Huadian at par value and the defendant was appointed a director of Huadian.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, prior to the commencement of proceedings in December 2007, the sequence of roles and actions undertaken by Sher Hock Guan Charles in relation to ABB entities, Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute, Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, Great Vision International Pte Ltd, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd, Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd, Gelpag International Pte Ltd and Gelpag mbH resulted in a conflict between his external business activities and his contractual and senior management obligations to the ABB Singapore Group companies.
Party_Details.Rule: An individual who holds senior management and board positions in a company is generally expected, under contract and general company law principles, to avoid external business activities that intersect with or compete against the company’s business, to refrain from using information or opportunities obtained through his position to advance separate ventures, and to disclose to his employer material information about external projects that may affect the employer’s commercial interests.
Party_Details.Application: While serving as President, director, General Manager and Vice-President within ABB Singapore Group companies, Sher Hock Guan Charles engaged with Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute about its plan to develop and market medium voltage switchgear and circuit breakers, introduced former ABB technical expert Guenther Leonhardt to that institute, and later joined and invested in Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, which manufactured and marketed medium voltage products internationally. During the same period he facilitated a property development project in Xiamen through Great Vision International Pte Ltd and Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd, received a substantial financial return from that project which was used to fund an equity stake in Huadian via Gelpag mbH, and assisted in the incorporation and directorship of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd and Gelpag International Pte Ltd. These pre-dispute events created a pattern in which his external ventures and investments intersected with the product and geographic markets the ABB Singapore Group companies for which he held senior roles.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examines the defendant’s career path within the ABB Group, his contractual terms, and the Employee Handbook to determine the scope of his obligations. It concludes that he was a very senior employee who owed fiduciary duties to ABB Industry Pte Ltd and fiduciary duties as a director to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd. The judgment finds that his assistance to Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute in recruiting Guenther Leonhardt for the development and commercialisation of medium voltage products, and his subsequent role and investment in Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, were inconsistent with his obligations to act in the best interests of the ABB companies and to avoid conflicts of interest. The court distinguishes between his involvement in the Xiamen property development project and Webmoney, which it treats as either not established as active participation during his ABB employment or as unrelated to ABB’s business, and his conduct relating to Huadian, which it treats as giving rise to liability. It ultimately holds that he is liable to ABB Industry Pte Ltd (and to a more limited extent to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd) for breaches of his contractual and fiduciary obligations, with the assessment of loss and quantum to be addressed in separate proceedings.
